content:
# Exodus Chapter 33 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 103–104; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 76–77 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_033_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_033_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_033_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_033_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### YHWH Orders Departure — But Will Not Go Among Them (vv. 1–3)

**1** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Leave this place — you and the people you brought up out of the land of Egypt — and go to the land I promised on oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob when I said, 'I will give it to your descendants.' **2** I will send an angel ahead of you, and I will drive out the Canaanites, the Amorites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites. **3** Go up to a land flowing with milk and honey. But I will not go up among you myself, because you are a stiff-necked people, and I might destroy you on the way."

---

### The People Mourn — Stripped of Ornaments (vv. 4–6)

**4** When the people heard this devastating word, they mourned, and no one put on any ornaments. **5** For YHWH had told Moses, "Say to the Sons of Israel: 'You are a stiff-necked people. If I were to go among you even for a single moment, I would destroy you. So now, take off your ornaments, and I will decide what to do with you.'" **6** So the Sons of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments from Mount Horeb onward.

---

### The Tent of Meeting — Outside the Camp (vv. 7–11)

**7** Now Moses would take the tent and pitch it outside the camp, at some distance away, and he called it the Tent of Meeting. Anyone who wanted to seek YHWH would go out to the Tent of Meeting, which was outside the camp. **8** Whenever Moses went out to the tent, all the people would rise and stand at the entrances of their own tents, watching Moses until he entered. **9** And whenever Moses entered the tent, the pillar of cloud would descend and stand at the entrance of the tent, and YHWH would speak with Moses. **10** When all the people saw the pillar of cloud standing at the entrance of the tent, they would all rise and worship, each at the entrance of their own tent. **11** YHWH would speak to Moses face to face, the way a person speaks to a friend. Then Moses would return to the camp, but his assistant Joshua son of Nun, a young man, would not leave the tent.

---

### Moses Pleads — "Show Me Your Way" (vv. 12–13)

**12** Moses said to YHWH, "Look — you keep telling me, 'Lead this people forward,' but you have not let me know whom you will send with me. Yet you yourself have said, 'I know you by name,' and, 'You have found favor in my eyes.' **13** So now, if I really have found favor in your eyes, please show me your ways, so that I may truly know you and continue to find favor in your eyes. And remember that this nation is your people."

---

### YHWH's Presence Promised (vv. 14–17)

**14** YHWH said, "My presence will go with you, and I will give you rest." **15** Moses replied, "If your presence does not go with us, do not send us up from here. **16** How will anyone ever know that I and your people have found favor in your eyes unless you go with us? That is what sets me and your people apart from every other people on the face of the earth."

**17** YHWH said to Moses, "I will do this very thing you have asked, because you have found favor in my eyes, and I know you by name."

---

### "Show Me Your Glory" — The Cleft in the Rock (vv. 18–23)

**18** Then Moses said, "Please, show me your glory."

**19** YHWH said, "I will cause all my goodness to pass before you, and I will proclaim the name 'YHWH' in your presence. I will show grace to whom I choose to show grace, and I will show compassion to whom I choose to show compassion." **20** But he said, "You cannot see my face, because no human being can see me and live."

**21** Then YHWH said, "Look — there is a place near me where you are to stand on the rock. **22** When my glory passes by, I will put you in a cleft of the rock and shield you with my hand until I have passed by. **23** Then I will take my hand away, and you will see my back — but my face must not be seen."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Verse 1 — "The people you brought up"
The Hebrew phrasing here is pointed: YHWH says to Moses "you brought up" (הֶעֱלִיתָ), distancing himself from the people after the golden calf rebellion (ch. 32). This is a deliberate shift from earlier passages where YHWH claims the exodus as his own act. The LXX preserves this distancing with ἀνεβίβασας. The dynamic rendering "the people you brought up" captures this emotional distance without requiring a footnote.

### Verse 3 — "I might destroy you on the way"
The Hebrew כִּלָּה carries the sense of total consumption or annihilation. YHWH's refusal to travel among them is not punishment but protection — his holy presence among a rebellious people would be lethal. The LXX uses ἐξαναλώσω σε (utterly consume you). "I might destroy you" conveys both the danger and the mercy embedded in the withdrawal.

### Verse 7 — "The Tent of Meeting"
The Hebrew אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד here refers not to the Tabernacle (not yet constructed) but to a provisional tent Moses pitched outside the camp as a place of divine encounter. The LXX calls it ἡ σκηνή, with the note that it was set up ἔξω τῆς παρεμβολῆς (outside the camp). Its placement outside the camp dramatizes the rupture between YHWH and the people — the one who was to dwell among them (25:8) now meets them only at a distance.

### Verse 11 — "Face to face, the way a person speaks to a friend"
The Hebrew פָּנִים אֶל־פָּנִים is a relational idiom meaning direct, unmediated communication — not necessarily a visual experience. The LXX renders it ἐνώπιος ἐνωπίῳ (face-to-face). The paradox of the chapter is that Moses speaks to God "face to face" (v. 11) yet cannot see God's face (v. 20). The dynamic rendering adds "the way a person speaks to a friend" (from כַּאֲשֶׁר יְדַבֵּר אִישׁ אֶל־רֵעֵהוּ) to clarify that intimacy, not sight, is the point.

### Verse 14 — "My presence"
The Hebrew פָּנַי literally means "my face" — the very thing Moses is told he cannot see (v. 20). The wordplay is profound: YHWH's "face" will go with them, yet his "face" cannot be seen. The LXX uses αὐτὸς προπορεύσομαι (I myself will go before), losing this wordplay. "My presence" is the best dynamic equivalent in English, capturing the theological meaning — God's personal, active accompaniment — while "face" is preserved in the notes.

### Verse 19 — "I will cause all my goodness to pass before you"
Moses asked for כְּבוֹד (glory, v. 18), and YHWH answers with טוּב (goodness, v. 19). This is not a lesser substitute but a theological redefinition: God's glory is his goodness. The LXX has παρελεύσομαι πρότερός σου τῇ δόξῃ μου (I will pass before you with my glory), collapsing the distinction. The Hebrew text's careful movement from "glory" to "goodness" to the proclamation of YHWH's name reveals that divine glory is not spectacle but character.

### Verse 19 — Sovereign grace
The phrase וְחַנֹּתִי אֶת־אֲשֶׁר אָחֹן (I will show grace to whom I choose to show grace) uses a cognate construction that emphasizes divine freedom. Grace and compassion are not responses to human merit; they flow from YHWH's sovereign choice. The LXX preserves this structure: ἐλεήσω ὃν ἂν ἐλεῶ. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 9:15 as the definitive statement of divine sovereignty in mercy.

### Verses 21–23 — The cleft, the hand, the back
Three Hebrew terms carry the theological weight: נִקְרַת הַצּוּר (cleft of the rock), כַּפִּי (my palm/hand), and אֲחֹרָי (my back/aftereffects). The "hand" that shields is כַּף — the open palm, suggesting both protection and tenderness. The "back" (אֲחֹרָי) may suggest that humans can only perceive God after he has acted — we see the wake of his passing, never the oncoming fullness. The LXX uses τὰ ὀπίσω μου (what is behind me). This passage became foundational for Jewish and Christian theology of indirect divine knowledge — we know God through his effects, his character revealed in history, not through unmediated vision.

### Vaticanus Divergences
The LXX in Vaticanus generally follows the Hebrew closely in this chapter but shows notable tendencies: (1) In v. 14, the LXX renders YHWH's promise as "I myself will go before you" (αὐτὸς προπορεύσομαι) rather than "my face will go," losing the face/presence wordplay central to the Hebrew. (2) In v. 19, the LXX inserts "glory" (δόξῃ) where the Hebrew has "goodness" (טוּב), harmonizing Moses' request with YHWH's answer and losing the deliberate theological reframing. (3) In v. 5, Vaticanus reads the stripping of ornaments as a present command, while the Hebrew verb form suggests an ongoing condition "from Mount Horeb onward." In all cases, the Hebrew is followed as the primary text for this translation.
